title: Jerk pork shoulder
subtitle: Slow-roasted Jamaican jerk pork
excerpt: Bone-in pork shoulder marinated overnight in a scotch bonnet, allspice, and thyme paste, then slow-roasted until the meat pulls from the bone and the outside has blackened and crisped. The jerk marinade is a deeply fragrant mix that carries the heat of the scotch bonnet and the warm resinous note of allspice. Not a quick dish — the marinade needs overnight and the roast takes four hours.
sourceNotes: Jerk seasoning originated with the Maroons of Jamaica — formerly enslaved people who escaped to the Blue Mountains and preserved their meat using allspice (pimento) wood smoke and scotch bonnet peppers. The technique and flavour profile are documented from the 17th century. Pork is the original jerk meat; chicken became the more widely available version in the 20th century.

recipe.freezeNotes: Pull the pork before freezing. Reheat with a splash of water to prevent it drying out.
recipe.batchNotes: Cook a larger shoulder without changing the method. The roasting time increases only slightly.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Overnight marinade is essential. The allspice and scotch bonnet need at least 8 hours to penetrate the meat.

content.text: Bone-in pork shoulder gives the best result. The bone keeps the meat moist during the long roast and the marbled fat bastes the meat from the inside. A boneless shoulder can be used but the texture is different and the cook time should be reduced by 30 minutes.

Authentic jerk is cooked over pimento wood on a steel drum grill. Without that kit, a 160°C oven for four hours produces tender meat with a good crust. If you have a barbecue with a lid, finish the pork over indirect heat for the last 30 minutes to get the char.

Blend the {{scotch-bonnet}}, {{allspice}}, {{spring-onion}}, {{garlic}}, {{ginger-root}}, {{thyme}}, {{browning-sauce}}, {{rum-dark}}, lime juice, {{cinnamon-ground}}, {{nutmeg}}, {{black-pepper}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, and {{vegetable-oil}} to a rough paste in a blender or food processor. Score the {{pork-shoulder}} deeply all over, cutting through the fat cap to the meat. Push the marinade into the cuts and rub it over the entire surface. Cover and refrigerate for at least 8 hours.

Remove the pork from the fridge 30 minutes before cooking. Heat the oven to 160°C. Place the pork skin-side up on a rack over a roasting tray with a cup of water in the bottom to prevent the drippings burning. Roast for 4 hours, basting with the pan juices every hour. The outside should be deeply browned and almost charred in places. Rest for 20 minutes before pulling the meat from the bone. Serve with rice and peas and festival dumplings.

items.cause: Too much moisture in the oven or skin not scored deeply enough
items.fix: Increase the oven to 220°C for the final 20 minutes after removing the water from the tray. The skin needs dry high heat

items.cause: Shoulder too lean or oven too hot
items.fix: Bone-in shoulder should be self-basting. Add more water to the tray and cover loosely with foil if the surface is over-charring before the meat is tender

items.cause: Too few scotch bonnets or marinade time too short
items.fix: Jerk marinade fades over a long roast. For maximum flavour, brush additional marinade onto the pork at the 2-hour mark

content.text: Jerk pork is the original jerk. In Jamaica it is the food of the Blue Mountains and the Maroon communities of Accompong and Moore Town, cooked over pimento wood that adds a layer of smoke no marinade can replicate. At Boston Bay in Portland parish, the roadside jerk stalls have been serving pork from half-drum grills since the mid-20th century. In Britain, jerk chicken has eclipsed jerk pork in restaurants — but the pork version is the older dish and the richer one.
